content.text: Status Page Update Cadence During Incidents

A status page is a contract with users. When an incident is active, that contract demands regular, predictable updates—regardless of whether anything has changed technically. Teams that post status updates only when they have something new to say discover that users fill information gaps with their worst-case assumptions: the team doesn't know what happened, the service is permanently broken, nobody is working on it. Cadenced updates signal active engagement even when the root cause is still unknown.

Status page update cadence defines when updates are posted and what they must contain at each stage of the incident lifecycle. The cadence is not tied to discovery of new information—it is tied to elapsed time. Users and support teams need to know at regular intervals that the incident is being actively worked and that the status page is the authoritative source of truth.

if the update cadence timer fires and nothing new has been discovered, post 'Incident update: investigation continues, no new findings yet, next update in X minutes.' Silence is worse than a 'nothing new' update.

attrs.alt: Status page state machine: Investigating → Identified → Monitoring → Resolved with cadences
attrs.title: Status Page Update State Machine

content.text: Within 5 min of resolution + postmortem link within 5 days
Posted within 5 minutes of declaring a SEV-1. The team likely has no root cause yet. That's acceptable—the goal of this update is to acknowledge the issue and set update expectations.

Title: Elevated Error Rates — Inference API
Status: Investigating

Body:
We are investigating elevated error rates for requests to our Inference API.
Some users may experience failed responses or increased latency.
We will provide an update within 15 minutes as our investigation continues.
Posted when the root cause is confirmed. Include what the cause is (without revealing internal architecture detail), what is being done, and an ETA if one can be given responsibly.

Title: Inference API Degradation — Root Cause Identified
Status: Identified

Body:
We have identified the cause of elevated error rates: a configuration change
deployed at 14:30 UTC introduced increased connection pool exhaustion under
sustained load. We are rolling back the configuration.
Estimated restoration: 30-45 minutes.
Next update in 15 minutes or sooner if status changes.
Posted when the mitigation has been applied and metrics are returning to normal, but the team has not yet declared full resolution. Sets expectations before the final resolved update.

Title: Inference API — Recovery in Progress
Status: Monitoring

Body:
The configuration rollback has been applied. Error rates are returning to
normal levels. We are monitoring to confirm full stability before declaring
resolution. Affected period: 14:30–15:45 UTC.
The final status page entry. Must include the exact resolution time, a brief non-technical description of the impact, and a commitment to publish a postmortem.

Title: Inference API Incident — Resolved
Status: Resolved

Body:
This incident has been resolved. The Inference API returned to normal
operation at 15:58 UTC.

Impact: Users experienced elevated error rates (up to 34% of requests
failing) for approximately 88 minutes between 14:30 and 15:58 UTC. 
Requests during this period may need to be retried.

We are conducting a full postmortem and will publish our findings and
preventive actions within 5 business days at:
https://status.company.com/incidents/2026-07-14-inference-api
Manually remembering to post updates every 15 minutes during a high-stress incident fails regularly. Automate cadence reminders and, where possible, templated drafts:

Post an out-of-cadence update immediately when the incident state changes materially: severity escalation, new customer impact discovered, ETA changes significantly, or resolution declared. Don't wait for the next scheduled interval.

Planned maintenance with prior notice follows a different template than incident updates. Do not use incident terminology ('We are investigating') in maintenance windows—it creates confusion about whether something unexpected happened.

When two incidents are active simultaneously, create separate status page entries for each—even if they share root cause. Users need to know which service affects them. Cross-link the entries if they are related.
Track the following metrics in your incident review process:
time from incident declaration to first status page post. Target: under 5 minutes for SEV-1.
percentage of intervals where an update was posted within 2 minutes of the scheduled time. Target: above 90% for SEV-1.
time from technical resolution to final status page update. Target: under 5 minutes.
percentage of SEV-1 and SEV-2 resolved entries that link to a postmortem within 5 business days. Target: 100%.
Describe impact in terms of user experience, not AI behavior.
'The model is generating low-quality responses' is not useful. 'Users may receive responses that are incomplete or do not match their request' tells users what to expect and what to do.
Avoid attributing root cause to the model prematurely.

GenAI incidents have many potential causes: prompt changes, model version changes, retrieval issues, infrastructure degradation, or provider outages. Don't say 'model error' until the cause is confirmed.
Link to workarounds when they exist.

If users can work around the degradation (e.g., try a different prompt format, use an alternative endpoint), include that in the status update. GenAI users are often more technically able to apply workarounds than typical SaaS users.

The choice of status page platform affects what cadence automation is possible and how users receive updates. Evaluate platforms on three dimensions: API for programmatic updates, subscriber notification support, and history visibility.

The API is non-negotiable for teams that automate incident management. Without an API, posting updates requires manual login to the status page during an active incident—a source of delay and human error.
Status page platforms allow users to subscribe to notifications. The subscription strategy determines how effective the status page is as a communication channel.
Link to status page subscription from the product dashboard, API documentation, and support email footer. Users who are subscribed receive updates automatically and are less likely to flood support.
Let users subscribe to specific components (API, dashboard, webhooks). Enterprise customers who only use the API don't need notifications about dashboard incidents.
Offer a Slack webhook integration for enterprise tier customers so status page updates post directly into their ops channels. This reduces the lag between posting and customers seeing the update.
Enterprise API customers may want to consume status updates programmatically to integrate into their own incident dashboards. Provide webhook endpoints for this use case.

The IC is running the bridge call and managing action items. If the IC also drafts status page updates, one of these responsibilities degrades. Designate a separate Comms Lead for all SEV-1 incidents.
Status page not matching actual status.

The status page still shows 'Investigating' two hours into a mitigated incident. Users see 'Investigating' and assume the team is still lost. Update to 'Monitoring' or 'Resolved' the moment the technical status changes.

Marking an incident resolved before error rates have returned to baseline baseline to show the incident is short. Users who continue to experience issues see 'Resolved' and are confused or angry. Wait for confirmed metric stability before posting resolved.
The status page entry lists 'API' as affected but the dashboard and webhooks are also degraded. Users affected by dashboard issues find no explanation. Always enumerate all affected components.

For teams operating at scale, managing status page configuration—components, groups, and incident templates—through the platform UI becomes a maintenance burden. Treating the status page configuration as code, version-controlled and deployed through automation, improves consistency and enables rapid incident response.

This client is called from the war room bot or incident management automation. For SEV-1 incidents, the bot creates the incident page entry immediately on declaration and posts cadenced updates from the scheduler without requiring manual Comms Lead action.

Status page history is a trust-building asset. Teams that maintain a complete, accurate history of incidents—including ones that are embarrassing—build credibility with users. Teams that selectively remove or understate incidents destroy it when users notice the inconsistency.
Users bookmark incident pages. Deleted entries are noticed and generate trust loss disproportionate to whatever the original incident caused.
Correct errors by amendment, not deletion.
If an update contained incorrect information (wrong ETA, wrong root cause), post a correction update explaining the change. Don't edit the original silently.
After the postmortem is published, update the resolved incident entry to include the postmortem link. This creates a complete record from incident to root cause to preventive action.

Many status page platforms calculate and display monthly uptime based on incident history. Publishing this metric publicly holds the team accountable and provides customers with objective reliability data.

# status-page-cadence-runbook.yaml
status_page:
  sev1:
    first_update_deadline_minutes: 5
    update_interval_minutes: 15
    resolution_update_deadline_minutes: 5
    postmortem_deadline_days: 5

  sev2:
    first_update_deadline_minutes: 15
    update_interval_minutes: 30
    resolution_update_deadline_minutes: 10
    postmortem_deadline_days: 10

  sev3:
    first_update_deadline_minutes: 60  # optional
    update_on_state_change_only: true
    resolution_update_required: true

  comms_ownership:
    draft: comms_lead
    approve: ic
    post: comms_lead
    legal_gate_required_for:
      - data_exposure
      - safety_incident
      - sla_breach

  content_rules:
    - describe_user_impact_not_internal_cause
    - do_not_speculate_on_root_cause
    - include_affected_components_explicitly
    - include_eta_only_when_confident
    - always_include_next_update_time

  resolution_requirements:
    - confirmed_metric_stability: true
    - minimum_monitoring_window_minutes: 15
    - postmortem_link_in_final_update: true
    - affected_period_stated: true
Post the first status page update within 5 minutes for SEV-1—even with 'Investigating' and no root cause. The first update is the most important for user trust.
Update on a fixed timer, not on new information. A 'nothing new' update at the scheduled interval is better than silence.
Automate cadence reminders to the Comms Lead. Don't rely on memory during high-stress incident response.
Every resolved incident must link to a postmortem. That commitment, included in the final status page update, signals that the team takes reliability seriously and is learning from failures.
